text: Hands down, one of my favorite operations is a robotic ascending colectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. And in this video, I want to cover the seventeen logical steps that I use to execute this operation in an extremely time-efficient fashion.

For those who don't know me, I'm Dr. Mark Solomon, System Chairman of Colorectal Surgery for AdventHealth Orlando and Program Director for the Minimally Invasive Colorectal Surgical Fellowship at the Colon and Rectal Clinic of Orlando. All right, so let's get down to the meat of this. The first step of this operation, obviously, is positioning the patient. Now, this might seem to be a trivial thing that may be delegated to nursing and anesthesia teams.

Second step, access and insufflate the abdomen by whichever method you're comfortable with. I typically do an optical entry technique, but you can do really whatever you'd like. Step three, place ports. I'll link to a video that covers in exhaustive detail all the different possible port configurations that you can use to get this operation done. Step four, explore the abdomen. Step five, position the bowel.

I'm gonna pause here and just suggest one thing. Don't even look at the robot until the bowel has been correctly positioned prior to beginning the operation. Once you dock the robot, it is extremely difficult to position the small bowel any further, so I highly suggest that this part of the operation be done laparoscopically prior to docking of the robot.

Step six, initiate dissection. Now you can of course do this in the traditional lateral medial fashion, but I'll highlight in a different video the exact methods by which I do this in a medial to lateral fashion. Step seven, identify and preserve the duodenum, followed by step eight, identification, skeletonization, and division of the ileocolic pedicle. I typically do this with a vessel scaler, but certainly you can do that with any division technique that you like.

Step eight, medial to lateral mobilization, which is my preferred method. But of course, if you prefer a lateral to medial mobilization, you can do so. But to follow these seventeen steps, I use a medial to lateral mobilization. Step ten, lateral mobilization. Once the complete medial mobilization has occurred, followed by step eleven, which is division of the terminal ileal mesentery as well as division of the terminal ileum.

And I do this, of course, with a robotic stapler. most commonly on a blue load. Step twelve, division of the transverse mesocolon and transverse colon, followed by step thirteen, placement of the specimen above the right lobe of the liver. Trust me, place the specimen above the right lobe of the liver. Don't even bother putting it in your pelvis, because I've done that and nearly lost the specimen before. So placement of the specimen above the right lobe of the liver is safe. It's very easy to find that specimen at the end of the case. You don't have to go searching in the pelvis for it afterwards.

Step fourteen, performance of the ileocolic anastomosis. Now, there are countless methods by which this anastomosis can be performed, and there is really no one single right way to do it. There is a wrong way to do it, and I will link a video on how exactly not to do this anastomosis, but please peruse the channel for countless other ways to perform this ileocolic anastomosis in a safe manner.

Step fifteen, I now assess the perfusion of the anastomosis and interrogate it with ICG. And in step sixteen, extraction of the specimen, most commonly now in a specimen bag. Now, I normally extract the specimen through a phanacetal incision, but if the patients are a little bit larger and it's a challenging case that I don't want to fight a big panus through a phanacetal position, I'll typically extend the stapler port to finally extract the specimen. And the final step, which is seventeen, which is closure of the patient.
